lines.greek: ὁππότʼ ἂν ἡβήσῃ τε καὶ ἧς ἱμείρεται αἴης.
ὣς ἔφαθʼ Ἑρμείας, ἀλλʼ οὐ φρένας Αἰγίσθοιο
πεῖθʼ ἀγαθὰ φρονέων· νῦν δʼ ἁθρόα πάντʼ ἀπέτισεν.
τὸν δʼ ἠμείβετʼ ἔπειτα θεά, γλαυκῶπις Ἀθήνη·
ὦ πάτερ ἡμέτερε Κρονίδη, ὕπατε κρειόντων,
καὶ λίην κεῖνός γε ἐοικότι κεῖται ὀλέθρῳ·
ὡς ἀπόλοιτο καὶ ἄλλος, ὅτις τοιαῦτά γε ῥέζοι·
ἀλλά μοι ἀμφʼ Ὀδυσῆι δαΐφρονι δαίεται ἦτορ,
δυσμόρῳ, ὃς δὴ δηθὰ φίλων ἄπο πήματα πάσχει
νήσῳ ἐν ἀμφιρύτῃ, ὅθι τʼ ὀμφαλός ἐστι θαλάσσης.
νῆσος δενδρήεσσα, θεὰ δʼ ἐν δώματα ναίει,
Ἄτλαντος θυγάτηρ ὀλοόφρονος, ὅς τε θαλάσσης
πάσης βένθεα οἶδεν, ἔχει δέ τε κίονας αὐτὸς
μακράς, αἳ γαῖάν τε καὶ οὐρανὸν ἀμφὶς ἔχουσιν.
τοῦ θυγάτηρ δύστηνον ὀδυρόμενον κατερύκει,
αἰεὶ δὲ μαλακοῖσι καὶ αἱμυλίοισι λόγοισιν
θέλγει, ὅπως Ἰθάκης ἐπιλήσεται· αὐτὰρ Ὀδυσσεύς,
ἱέμενος καὶ καπνὸν ἀποθρῴσκοντα νοῆσαι
ἧς γαίης, θανέειν ἱμείρεται. οὐδέ νυ σοί περ
ἐντρέπεται φίλον ἦτορ, Ὀλύμπιε. οὔ νύ τʼ Ὀδυσσεὺς

interlinear: whenever he-comes-of-age and of-his-own longs land.
So spoke Hermes, but not the-mind of-Aegisthus
he-persuaded, good though-intending; and-now all-together all he-has-paid.
him then answered the-goddess, bright-eyed Athena:
'O father our, son-of-Cronos, highest of-rulers,
indeed exceedingly that-one lies in-fitting destruction;
so may-perish also another, whoever such-things does.
But for-me over Odysseus wise-hearted is-torn the-heart,
ill-fated, who indeed long from-friends sufferings suffers
in-an-island sea-girt, where the-navel is of-the-sea.
an-island wooded, and-a-goddess in-it halls inhabits,
of-Atlas the-daughter, baleful-minded, who of-the-sea
all the-depths knows, and-holds the-pillars himself,
tall, which earth and heaven apart hold.
his daughter the-wretched-one, lamenting, keeps-back,
and-ever with-soft and with-wheedling words
she-beguiles, that Ithaca he-may-forget; but Odysseus,
yearning even the-smoke leaping-up to-behold
of-his-own land, to-die longs. Yet not even for-you
is-moved the-dear heart, Olympian. Did-not Odysseus
literal: when he comes to manhood and longs for his own land.'
So Hermes spoke, but he did not persuade the mind of Aegisthus,
for all his good intent; and now he has paid the whole price at once."
Then the goddess, bright-eyed Athena, answered him:
'Our Father, son of Cronos, highest of lords,
truly that man lies in a destruction that is his due;
so may any other also perish who does such deeds.
But my heart is torn for wise-hearted Odysseus,
ill-fated man, who far from his friends has long suffered woes
on a sea-girt island, where lies the navel of the sea.
It is a wooded isle, and a goddess dwells there in her halls,
the daughter of baleful-minded Atlas, who knows
the depths of every sea, and himself holds the tall pillars
that keep earth and heaven apart.
His daughter it is who keeps back that wretched, grieving man,
and ever with soft and wheedling words
she beguiles him, that he may forget Ithaca. But Odysseus,
longing to see were it but the smoke leaping up
from his own land, yearns to die. And yet your
heart pays it no heed, Olympian. Did not Odysseus
translations.elizabethan:
—when he is grown a man and yearns for home."
So Hermes spake; yet all his fair intent
swayed not the mind of Aegisthus; and now
at one full reckoning he hath paid for all.
Then answered him the goddess, bright of eye,
Athena: "Father, son of Cronos, thou
most high of all that rule, right well that man
lies in the ruin that his deeds have earned—
so perish any other who does thus!
But for Odysseus is my heart torn through,
the wise, the luckless, who this weary while,
far from his own, endures his griefs, hemmed round
by sea upon an isle, earth's very navel.
A wooded isle it is; a goddess dwells
within its halls, the child of Atlas, he
of baleful mind, who knows the deep sea's floor
and bears the pillars, tall, that hold apart
the earth and heaven. His daughter keeps him there,
the wretched, weeping man, and evermore
with soft, beguiling words she casts her spell
to make him quite forget his Ithaca.
Yet he, Odysseus, longing but to see
the smoke leap upward from his native ground,
would gladly die. And is thy heart not moved,
Olympian, even now? Did not the man—"
translations.modernist:
—once he is a man and wants his own land back."

That is what Hermes told him. But Aegisthus would not listen, for all the good sense in it. Now he has paid for everything at once.

Then bright-eyed Athena answered. "Father, son of Cronos, highest of the lords. That man got the death he had coming. Let anyone die like that who does what he did. But my heart aches for Odysseus. He is wise and he has no luck. He has been far from his friends a long time now, suffering. He is on an island, ringed by sea, at the sea's center. It is wooded. A goddess lives there. She is the daughter of Atlas, who knows the depths of every sea and holds the tall pillars that keep earth and sky apart. His daughter keeps Odysseus there. He is wretched and he grieves. She talks to him, soft and coaxing, so he will forget Ithaca. But Odysseus wants only to see the smoke rising from his own land. Then he could die. And your heart does not care, Olympian. Did Odysseus not—"
translations.storybook:
—as soon as he grows up and longs to see his home again."

That is what kind Hermes told Aegisthus. But Aegisthus would not listen, even though it was very good advice indeed—and now he has had to pay for all his wickedness at once.

Then the goddess Athena, with her bright, clever eyes, spoke up. "Father Zeus," she said, "highest of all the gods, that man got exactly what he deserved, and so should anyone who behaves so badly! But oh, my heart aches for clever Odysseus. Poor, unlucky man! For such a long, long time he has been far away from everyone he loves, and so unhappy. He is stuck on an island, right in the middle of the wide sea. It is a lovely wooded island, but a goddess lives there. She is the daughter of Atlas—the giant who knows all the deep places of the ocean and holds up the great tall pillars that keep the earth and the sky apart. It is his daughter who keeps Odysseus there. Every single day she whispers sweet, coaxing words to him, hoping he will forget his home in Ithaca. But all Odysseus wants is to catch just one glimpse of the smoke rising from his own chimney—and then he would be happy to die. Doesn't that make your heart sad at all, Zeus? Didn't Odysseus—"

murray.text: “Father of us all, thou son of Cronos, high above all lords, aye, verily that man lies low in a destruction that is his due; so, too, may any other also be destroyed who does such deeds. But my heart is torn for wise Odysseus, hapless man, who far from his friends has long been suffering woes

in a sea-girt isle, where is the navel of the sea. 'Tis a wooded isle, and therein dwells a goddess, daughter of Atlas of baneful mind, who knows the depths of every sea, and himself holds the tall pillars which keep earth and heaven apart.

His daughter it is that keeps back that wretched, sorrowing man; and ever with soft and wheedling words she beguiles him that he may forget Ithaca. But Odysseus, in his longing to see were it but the smoke leaping up from his own land, yearns to die. Yet thy

heart doth not regard it, Olympian. Did not Odysseus beside the ships of the Argives offer thee sacrifice without stint in the broad land of Troy? Wherefore then didst thou conceive such wrath against him, O Zeus?” Then Zeus, the cloud-gatherer, answered her and said: “My child, what a word has escaped the barrier of thy teeth?
